title: e2e: ExitCleanly(): a few more
body:
Continuing progress on RUN-1907

The second commit is the important one to review.
```release-note
None
```

comments.body: Some test unhappiness.  I don't have the answer to your question, but the rest of the code LGTM.

@vrothberg I can't squash, because I'm not a sadist. My process in this entire `ExitCleanly()` exercise is to produce reviewable PRs, and the way to do that is (1) first commit is enormous and unreviewable, (2) second commit is the important stuff that deserves attention.

This will not affect `git bisect` because that's not how `git bisect` works. We always merge with `--no-ff`, so multiple commits in a PR are never seen on the `main` (or maint) branch itself.

> This will not affect `git bisect` because that's not how `git bisect` works. We always merge with `--no-ff`, so multiple commits in a PR are never seen on the `main` (or maint) branch itself.

So `git bisect` will never hit the first commit?  That sounds surprising to me.  I know that `git bisect` is necessarily able to traverse the tree correctly but I'd be surprised if it wasn't able to jump exactly on this commit during a bisect and get noise.

> This will not affect git bisect because that's not how git bisect works. We always merge with --no-ff, so multiple commits in a PR are never seen on the main (or maint) branch itself.

I cannot talk to git bisect but each individual commits end up in main so yes we see them in main.
`git log --oneline --author=Ed`

> I cannot talk to git bisect but each individual commits end up in main so yes we see them in main.
> `git log --oneline --author=Ed`

Yes, to my understanding `no-ff` just makes sure to always create a merge commit.

I just want to make sure that tests pass on each commit. `break + something + fix` is very time-consuming and frustrating when doing a `git bisect` and running specific tests to find a bug-introducing commit.

Well, phooey. All these years I've been wrong about git-bisect. I will forevermore use `--first-parent` because that is the correct way for it to work.

I'm at a loss as to how to proceed here. I could spend a few more hours hand-editing the first commit so tests pass. That feels like make-work. I could squash commits, that would be criminal; unspeakably cruel to reviewers and to future spelunkers. 

@vrothberg could I ask you to think about what you're asking, including following through on how this could ever affect a real-world `git bisect`? I argue that it is impossible for this to be a problem IRL:  it would require someone to use e2e tests as a `git bisect run` filter, and that would be impossible: PRs do not get merged unless tests pass, so there could never be a `bad` commit. (If there is, we notice immediately). We (at least I) only ever use `git bisect run some-new-test-that-we've-never-run-before` because that's what breaks: something we've never tested.

Anyhow, I need to figure out how to solve the CNI nslookup breakage before I proceed. I hope we can reach a common ground on how to work this PR and future ones.

> I'm at a loss as to how to proceed here. I could spend a few more hours hand-editing the first commit so tests pass. That feels like make-work. I could squash commits, that would be criminal; unspeakably cruel to reviewers and to future spelunkers.

I'd be OK with squashing but I won't block, so consider this a LGTM :)

> @vrothberg could I ask you to think about what you're asking, including following through on how this could ever affect a real-world `git bisect`? I argue that it is impossible for this to be a problem IRL: it would require someone to use e2e tests as a `git bisect run` filter, and that would be impossible: PRs do not get merged unless tests pass, so there could never be a `bad` commit.

While PRs can only merge when green, we do not test each commit individually within a PR.  So it's possible that a commit breaks certain tests which a subsequent commit then addresses/fixes.

I rarely run `git bisect` when analyzing things/debugging but I am sure to have run all kinds of system and e2e tests.  So this commit in question can cause noise / false positives during such a bisect.  I usually run `git bisect` manually, then compile podman to eventually run the system/e2e tests or any other reproducer I may have at hand.
@vrothberg I've settled for `Skip()` on commit 1, removed in commit 2. This will preserve `git-bisect`. Will that meet your requirements?

> @vrothberg I've settled for `Skip()` on commit 1, removed in commit 2. This will preserve `git-bisect`. Will that meet your requirements?

Absolutely, thanks a lot, @edsantiago !
